steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: This is a Boundaries question testing punctuation between two main clauses. The first clause ends with the blank: "cells with identical DNA develop different [blank]". The second clause ("whereas the genome... will be expressed") explains why that happens. The correct punctuation must both separate the two clauses and signal that an explanation follows.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — functions: No punctuation between the two independent clauses creates a run-on sentence. ✗
  • Choice B — functions,: A comma alone between two independent clauses is a comma splice. ✗
  • Choice C — functions and,: Placing the comma after "and" rather than before it is ungrammatical. More importantly, "and" signals addition, not explanation — it fails to convey that the second clause explains the first. ✗
  • Choice D — functions:: A colon after a complete independent clause correctly introduces an explanation or elaboration. Here it signals that the "whereas" clause explains how cells with identical DNA can develop different functions. ✓
That is the correct answer.
